name: Śrīdhara
dates_notes: Conventionally dated c. 950–1000 CE on the basis of internal evidence in the *Nyāya-Kandalī* — Śrīdhara dates the work to Śaka 913 (= 991 CE) in the colophon — and the citation chain (he cites Prabhākara, Kumārila, and the Buddhist epistemologists; he is in turn cited by Udayana). Sastri 1969 edition introduction; Halbfass 1992 (On Being and What There Is, the Vaiśeṣika chapter).
core_thesis: Śrīdhara is the principal commentator on Praśastapāda's *Padārtha-Dharma-Saṃgraha* and the author of the most influential mature Vaiśeṣika treatise of the late tenth century. The *Nyāya-Kandalī* (Marsh-Plant of Nyāya, named after the wetland flower that grows abundantly in Bengal where Śrīdhara composed the work) is a sustained prose commentary on Praśastapāda that is also an independent philosophical treatise. The treatise's principal contributions are three. First, it supplies the canonical mature articulation of Vaiśeṣika ontology under the six (or seven, counting *abhāva*) padārthas — *dravya* (substance), *guṇa* (quality), *karma* (action), *sāmānya* (universality), *viśeṣa* (particularity), *samavāya* (inherence), and *abhāva* (absence) — with extended treatment of the more difficult technical issues (the analysis of *samavāya* as a single eternal entity; the analysis of *viśeṣa* as the differentiating principle within the eternal substances; the analysis of *abhāva* under the four sub-types *prāgabhāva*, *pradhvaṃsābhāva*, *anyonyābhāva*, *atyantābhāva*). Second, it supplies the Vaiśeṣika engagement with the rival darśanas of its period — the Buddhist analysis of universals (the Vaiśeṣika defense of real *sāmānya* against the Buddhist *apoha*), the Mīmāṃsā analysis of negation (the Vaiśeṣika defense of *abhāva* as a real padārtha against the Mīmāṃsā reduction of negation to a positive cognition), and the Sāṅkhya analysis of causality (the Vaiśeṣika defense of *asat-kārya-vāda* — the doctrine that the effect is not pre-existent in the cause — against the Sāṅkhya *sat-kārya-vāda*). Third, it supplies a sustained theistic articulation of Vaiśeṣika ontology: Īśvara is the efficient cause of the periodic cosmic creation and dissolution, and the standard Vaiśeṣika categorial scheme is integrated into a theistic cosmology. The treatise is the principal source for understanding mature pre-Udayana Vaiśeṣika and is the natural counterpart to Vyomaśiva's *Vyomavatī* and Udayana's *Kiraṇāvalī*.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Kandali
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Śrīdhara. The colophon dates the work to Śaka 913 (= 991 CE) and identifies Bhuriśreṣṭha (modern Bhurshut in West Bengal) as the place of composition. Sanskrit text edited by V. P. Dvivedin (1895, Vizianagaram Sanskrit Series), revised by Sastri (1969).

ascription_evidence.description: Sastri 1969 critical edition; the colophon's self-dating is the firmest single internal-dating in pre-Navya Indian philosophy.

engaged_works.summary: A complete prose commentary on Praśastapāda's *Padārtha-Dharma-Saṃgraha* (Compendium of the Properties of the Categories), the canonical Vaiśeṣika treatise of the early-to-mid first millennium. The commentary follows the structure of the root-text: an opening treatment of the foundational issues, then sustained analyses of each of the six padārthas. The treatment of *dravya* covers the nine substances (earth, water, fire, air, ether, time, space, self, manas) and articulates the standard Vaiśeṣika analysis of the four physical substances as composed of atoms (*paramāṇu*) that combine into dyads, triads, and macroscopic objects via the periodic creative agency of Īśvara. The treatment of *guṇa* covers the twenty-four qualities (with extended analyses of *jñāna* as a quality of the self and of *saṃyoga* as a quality of contact). The treatment of *karma* covers the five types of action. The treatment of *sāmānya* defends real universals against the Buddhist apoha-theory and against the Mīmāṃsā reduction. The treatment of *viśeṣa* defends the doctrine of *antya-viśeṣa* (ultimate particularizers) as the differentiating principle within the eternal substances. The treatment of *samavāya* defends inherence as a single eternal entity. The treatment of *abhāva* defends absence as a real padārtha. The treatise is the canonical mature Vaiśeṣika ontology and the principal source for the school's tenth-century position.

reception_notes: Śrīdhara's *Nyāya-Kandalī* is one of three principal commentaries on Praśastapāda — together with Vyomaśiva's *Vyomavatī* and Udayana's *Kiraṇāvalī* — and is the most extensively studied of the three in the subsequent commentarial tradition. The treatise's articulation of Vaiśeṣika as theistic and its engagement with the Buddhist analysis are particularly important for the later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika synthesis that Udayana consolidates and that the Navya-Nyāya tradition inherits. Sanskrit Vedāntic engagement with Vaiśeṣika ontology proceeds via the *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.2.11–17 critique of *paramāṇu-kāraṇa-vāda* (atomism as ultimate causal account); Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on this adhikaraṇa is the standard Vedāntic reference but does not name Śrīdhara directly.
